title: Implement sp wt rm command
description:
Remove worktree with optional branch cleanup.

Usage: sp wt rm <name> [options]

Behavior:
- Removes worktree directory via git worktree remove
- Optionally deletes local and/or remote branches

Flags:
- --force - Remove even if working directory is dirty
- --del-local - Also delete the local branch
- --del-remote - Also delete the remote branch  
- --del-all - Delete both local and remote branches

Error handling:
- Fail gracefully if worktree doesn't exist
- Warn (don't fail) if branch doesn't exist when --del-* specified

Reference: ~/dotfiles/bin/git-ht cmd_remove()

title: Add spinner and feedback while opening PRs for the first time
taspr-inmem-rewrite
description:
# In-Memory Message Rewrite

Implement message rewriting without touching the working directory.

## Function

```typescript
async function rewriteCommitMessage(
  commit: string,
  newMessage: string,
  options: GitOptions = {}
): Promise<string>
```

## Algorithm

1. Get tree from original commit (unchanged)
2. Get parents from original commit (unchanged)
3. Get author AND committer env (preserve both for message-only changes)
4. Create new commit with `git commit-tree`
5. Return new commit SHA

## Key Points
- Tree is reused (no content change)
- Both author and committer dates preserved (message-only = no timestamp update)
- No working directory access at all

## Usage Locations
- Add/remove/modify trailers (Taspr-Commit-Id, Taspr-Group)
- All operations in `group-rebase.ts` that only change messages

## Tests
- Verify tree SHA unchanged
- Verify author/committer info preserved
- Verify message correctly modified

title: Help text - comprehensive --help for all commands
description:
## Goal
Provide comprehensive, well-formatted help text for all commands using Commander\'s built-in help system.

## Commands to Document

### Main Command: `taspr`
```
taspr - Stacked PR management for GitHub

Usage: taspr <command> [options]

Commands:
  view      Show current stack structure and PR status
  sync      Sync local stack with GitHub (push branches, create/update PRs)
  land      Merge the bottom PR to main
  group     Manage commit groups (create, edit, dissolve)

Options:
  -h, --help     Show this help message
  -v, --version  Show version number

Examples:
  taspr view              Show stack structure
  taspr sync              Push and create PRs
  taspr sync --open       Push, create PRs, and open in browser
  taspr land              Merge bottom ready PR
  taspr group create      Create a new commit group

Run 'taspr <command> --help' for detailed help on each command.
```

### `taspr view`
```
Show the current stack structure and PR status

Usage: taspr view [options]

Options:
  --all          Show all stacks across branches (not just current)
  --json         Output as JSON for scripting
  -h, --help     Show this help message

The view command shows:
  - Commits in the current stack (between HEAD and origin/main)
  - Which commits are grouped into multi-commit PRs
  - PR numbers and their current status (draft, open, merged)
  - CI status, review status, and comment threads (if PRs exist)

Status indicators:
  ✓  Checks passing, approved
  ●  Checks running or pending review
  ✗  Checks failing or changes requested
  ◐  Draft PR

Examples:
  taspr view         Current branch stack
  taspr view --all   All branches with stacks
```

### `taspr sync`
```
Sync local stack with GitHub

Usage: taspr sync [options]

Options:
  --open         Open created PRs in browser
  --dry-run      Show what would be done without doing it
  -h, --help     Show this help message

The sync command:
  1. Adds Taspr-Commit-Id trailers to commits that don\'t have them
  2. Creates branches for each PR unit (single commit or group)
  3. Creates PRs on GitHub with correct stacking (each based on PR below)
  4. Updates existing PRs if commits have changed
  5. Rebases on origin/main if behind (with conflict detection)

Branch naming: <prefix>/<username>/<commit-id>
  Example: taspr/alice/a1b2c3d4

Each PR\'s base branch is the branch of the PR below it in the stack,
or main/master for the bottom PR.

Examples:
  taspr sync             Sync stack to GitHub
  taspr sync --open      Sync and open new PRs in browser
  taspr sync --dry-run   Preview sync operations
```

### `taspr land`
```
Merge PRs to main

Usage: taspr land [options]

Options:
  --all          Land all consecutive ready PRs (not just bottom)
  -h, --help     Show this help message

The land command merges the bottom PR in your stack to main/master.
GitHub automatically retargets child PRs to the new base after merge.

A PR is "ready" when:
  - CI checks are passing
  - Has required approvals
  - No unresolved review threads
  - Not a draft

Examples:
  taspr land         Merge bottom ready PR
  taspr land --all   Merge all consecutive ready PRs
```

### `taspr group`
```
Manage commit groups (multiple commits as one PR)

Usage: taspr group <subcommand> [options]

Subcommands:
  create    Create a new group from selected commits
  edit      Edit an existing group (title or range)
  dissolve  Remove a group, making commits individual PRs

By default, each commit becomes its own PR. Groups let you bundle
multiple related commits into a single PR while keeping them as
separate commits locally.

Groups use git trailers:
  - Taspr-Group-Start: <group-id>   (on first commit)
  - Taspr-Group-End: <group-id>     (on last commit)
  - Taspr-Group-Title: <title>      (on first commit)

Examples:
  taspr group create     Interactive commit selection
  taspr group edit       Edit existing group
  taspr group dissolve   Remove group, keep commits
```

## Implementation with Commander

```typescript
import { program } from 'commander';

program
  .name('taspr')
  .description('Stacked PR management for GitHub')
  .version('0.1.0');

program
  .command('view')
  .description('Show current stack structure and PR status')
  .option('--all', 'Show all stacks across branches')
  .option('--json', 'Output as JSON')
  .addHelpText('after', `
Status indicators:
  ✓  Checks passing, approved
  ●  Checks running or pending review
  ✗  Checks failing or changes requested
  ◐  Draft PR

Examples:
  taspr view         Current branch stack
  taspr view --all   All branches with stacks
`)
  .action(viewCommand);
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Main taspr --help shows all commands
- [ ] Each command has detailed --help
- [ ] Examples included for common use cases
- [ ] Status indicators explained in view help
- [ ] Group trailer format documented in group help
- [ ] Help text fits standard terminal width (80 chars)

title: Update GitHub Actions workflow
gh CLI dependency checks
description:
## Remaining Work

The core gh CLI check (`ensureGhInstalled`) exists and is called before all GitHub operations. What's missing:

### 1. Caching
Currently `ensureGhInstalled` checks `which gh` on every call. Should cache the result after first successful check.

```typescript
let ghChecked = false;

export async function ensureGhInstalled(): Promise<void> {
  if (ghChecked) return;
  // ... existing check ...
  ghChecked = true;
}
```

### 2. Explicit Authentication Check
Currently authentication failures are caught when API calls fail. Could add explicit `gh auth status` check for clearer error messages.

### 3. Repository Access Check
Could add check for `gh repo view` to detect when user doesn't have access to the current repo.

## What's Already Done
- ✅ `ensureGhInstalled()` function exists in `src/github/api.ts`
- ✅ Called before all GitHub operations in `pr.ts` and `view.ts`
- ✅ Clear installation instructions for macOS, Linux, other
- ✅ Error class `DependencyError` for missing gh
- ✅ Error class `GitHubAuthError` for auth failures

title: PR lookup by branch
Improve error messages with context and remediation steps

title: Interactive mode and --orphans flag for sp clean
description:
## Goal
Enhance `sp clean` with more sophisticated branch detection and an interactive mode.

## Background
Currently `sp clean` detects two types of branches:
1. **Safe**: Branches whose exact SHA is merged into main (fast-forward/rebase)
2. **Unsafe**: Branches matched only by Spry-Commit-Id trailer (squash/amended)

There are other scenarios that could leave orphaned branches:
- Branches with no local counterpart (remote-only spry branches)
- Branches whose PRs were closed without merging
- Branches from PRs that were superseded by rebases
- Branches from abandoned work

## Proposed Changes

### Interactive Mode (`-i` or `--interactive`)
Present branches in a TUI allowing user to:
- Review each branch with context (last commit date, PR status if any)
- Select which to delete
- See diff summary or commit log for uncertain cases

### Orphan Detection (`--orphans`)
Add detection for branches that:
- Have no local tracking branch
- Are owned by current user (match spry prefix pattern)
- May not have matching commits in main at all

This would require `--orphans --force` to actually delete, similar to `--unsafe`.

### Considerations
- What about branches from closed (not merged) PRs?
- Should we check PR status via GitHub API?
- How to handle branches from other users (probably never touch)
- Performance with many branches
- Should --orphans imply fetching fresh data?

title: Conflict recovery (continue after manual fix)
Flaky test: sp group --fix merge mode
description:
## Failed Test
```
sp group --fix > Explicit merge with --fix=merge
```

## How to Run
```bash
GITHUB_INTEGRATION_TESTS=1 bun test tests/integration/group-fix.test.ts -t 'Explicit merge'
```

## Error
```
tests/integration/group-fix.test.ts:130
expect(result.exitCode).toBe(0);

Expected: 0
Received: 1
```

## Analysis
The test runs `sp group --fix` with merge mode and expects success (exit code 0) but gets failure (exit code 1).

**Root cause suspicion:** 
1. The command is failing for an unknown reason - need to capture stderr/stdout from the failed run
2. Could be a race condition with git state or file system
3. Could be related to how the test sets up the problematic group state before running --fix

**Fix approach:** Add more logging to capture why the command failed. Check the test setup to ensure the group state is correctly invalid before running --fix merge.

## Location
tests/integration/group-fix.test.ts:130

title: Add --apply flag for sync --open
description:
Add a non-interactive way to specify which commits/groups should have PRs opened.

## Syntax
```bash
taspr sync --open --apply='["id1", "id2"]'
```

## Identifier Resolution
Each identifier in the array can be:
- Git commit hash (full or partial)
- Taspr-Commit-Id (the 8-char ID in trailers)
- Group ID

## Behavior
- Only opens PRs for the specified commits/groups
- All commits still get branches pushed (for stacking)
- Validates all identifiers exist before starting
- Skips identifiers that already have PRs (no error)

## Format
Simple JSON array of identifiers:
```json
["abc123", "def456", "group-id"]
```

## Error Cases
- Invalid JSON: "Error: Invalid --apply format. Expected JSON array of identifiers."
- Ambiguous identifier: "Error: 'abc' matches multiple commits. Please provide more characters."
- Not found: "Error: No commit or group matching 'xyz' found in stack"

## Implementation Notes
- This provides the data interface for the interactive mode (-i)
- Interactive mode will build this array through TUI selection
- Reuse identifier resolution logic from --up-to implementation
close_reason: Implemented --apply flag for sync --open. Added identifier module for resolving commits/groups by various identifiers.

title: Design better strategy for untitled groups
description:
## Problem

When a group is first created (commits with Spry-Group trailer), there's no title stored in ref storage yet. The current workaround uses `getUnitTitle()` to fall back to the first commit subject, but this is a band-aid fix.

## Solution

**Fail sync when creating PR without a stored group title.** Users can override with `--allow-untitled-pr` flag.

This approach:
- Makes the user aware they need to set a title
- Avoids PRs with unexpected/incorrect titles  
- Explicit opt-in for fallback behavior

## Implementation

1. Created `src/core/title.ts` with unified helpers:
   - `resolveUnitTitle(unit)` - consistent fallback logic
   - `hasStoredTitle(unit)` - check if title is stored

2. Added `--allow-untitled-pr` flag to sync command

3. Validation in sync.ts:
   - Before creating PR for group, check `hasStoredTitle()`
   - If no stored title and no flag, fail with helpful error
   - If `--allow-untitled-pr`, use `resolveUnitTitle()` and continue

4. Consolidated all scattered fallback logic to use `resolveUnitTitle()`

## Files Modified

- `src/core/title.ts` - New helper module
- `src/core/title.test.ts` - Unit tests (9 tests)
- `src/cli/commands/sync.ts` - Add validation and flag
- `src/cli/index.ts` - Add CLI option
- `src/cli/output.ts` - Use resolveUnitTitle()
- `src/cli/commands/land.ts` - Use resolveUnitTitle()
- `src/cli/commands/view.test.ts` - Updated test expectation
- `tests/integration/helpers.ts` - Added allowUntitledPr option
- `tests/integration/sync.test.ts` - Added untitled group test

## Acceptance Criteria

- [x] Create resolveUnitTitle() helper
- [x] Create hasStoredTitle() helper
- [x] Add unit tests for helpers
- [x] Add --allow-untitled-pr flag
- [x] Validate group titles in sync
- [x] Consolidate fallback logic
- [x] Add integration tests

All tests passing (386 unit, 4 PR body integration tests)

- Seeing which PRs would be created/updated
- Previewing rebase operations
- Understanding what branches would be pushed

Implementation notes:

- No actual git operations or GitHub API calls that modify state
- Read-only API calls (like checking PR status) are fine

title: Epic 3: Push Stack to GitHub as PRs
description:
## Goal
Create GitHub PRs for each PRUnit, with correct stacking (each PR based on the one below it).

## Background

### Stacked PRs Model
In a stack of 3 PRUnits (A, B, C from bottom to top):
- PR for A: base = main, head = branch for A
- PR for B: base = branch for A, head = branch for B
- PR for C: base = branch for B, head = branch for C

This creates a review chain where reviewers see only the changes in each PR, not accumulated changes.

### Branch Naming Convention
```
<prefix>/<username>/<pr-id>
```

Example: `taspr/msims/a1b2c3d4`

Where:
- `prefix`: Configurable via `git config taspr.branchPrefix` (default: "taspr")
- `username`: GitHub username from `gh api user`
- `pr-id`: The PRUnit's id (commit-id for singles, group-id for groups)

This naming:
- Is stable (ID survives rebases)
- Identifies the owner
- Groups all taspr branches with a common prefix

### No PR Metadata Storage
At this stage, we don't store PR numbers anywhere. We discover them by:
1. Looking up PRs by branch name
2. GitHub API: `gh pr list --head <branch>`

This keeps the design simple—git trailers remain the single source of truth.

## Workflow

1. User runs `taspr sync --open`
2. For each PRUnit without an existing PR:
   - Create/update branch: `git push origin <commit>:refs/heads/<branch-name>`
   - Create PR: `gh pr create --head <branch> --base <parent-branch>`
3. Report created PRs

## Deliverable
Running `taspr sync --open` creates branches and PRs on GitHub. Each PR's base is the branch of the PR below it (or main for the bottom).

## Dependencies
- Epic 2 must be complete (commits have IDs)

## Key Technical Decisions
- Use `gh` CLI for all GitHub operations (leverages user's existing auth)
- Branch per PRUnit, named by pr-id
- PRs discovered by branch lookup, not stored in trailers

## Testable Outcome
PRs appear on GitHub, correctly stacked, with titles from commit/group.

title: Epic 4: Update PRs After Local Changes
description:
## Goal
When commits change (amend, rebase), update the corresponding GitHub branches to keep PRs in sync.

## Background

### The Update Problem
After commits are amended or rebased:
- Commit hashes change
- Local branch diverges from remote
- PRs show stale code

taspr needs to detect these changes and force-push updated branches.

### How Changes Are Detected
Compare local commit hash to remote branch tip:
```bash
git rev-parse taspr/user/a1b2c3d4  # Remote branch tip
git rev-parse HEAD~2               # Local commit for that PRUnit
```

If they differ, the branch needs updating.

## Workflow

1. User amends a commit or rebases
2. User runs `taspr sync`
3. For each PRUnit:
   - Compare local commit to remote branch
   - If different, force-push the branch
4. Report updated branches/PRs

## Implementation Approach

### Change Detection
```typescript
async function getBranchCommit(branchName: string): Promise<string | null> {
  const result = await $\`git rev-parse origin/${branchName}\`.nothrow();
  return result.exitCode === 0 ? result.stdout.toString().trim() : null;
}

async function needsUpdate(unit: PRUnit, branchName: string): Promise<boolean> {
  const remote = await getBranchCommit(branchName);
  const local = unit.commits[unit.commits.length - 1];
  return remote !== local;
}
```

### Force Push
```typescript
async function updateBranch(commitHash: string, branchName: string): Promise<void> {
  await $\`git push --force origin ${commitHash}:refs/heads/${branchName}\`;
}
```

## Deliverable
Running `taspr sync` force-pushes branches where commit hashes changed, keeping PRs in sync with local state.

## Dependencies
- Epic 3 must be complete (branches and PRs exist)

## MVP Note
After Epic 4, you have a working MVP:
- Create commits with IDs
- Push as stacked PRs
- Update PRs when local commits change

## Testable Outcome
Amending a commit and running sync updates the PR on GitHub.
close_reason: All child tasks completed: change detection, force push, view command, and git config options are all implemented.

title: Prevent fixup!/amend! commits from auto-reordering during rebases
description:
## Problem
Git's `rebase --autosquash` automatically reorders `fixup!` and `amend!` commits to be placed after their target commits. This can interfere with our custom rebase operations in the group TUI and other places.

## Solution
Ensure all our rebasing operations (group rebase, sync rebase, etc.) do NOT use `--autosquash` or explicitly disable it with `--no-autosquash` to prevent unexpected commit reordering.

## Files to Check
- `src/git/group-rebase.ts` - group reordering rebases
- Any other files that invoke `git rebase`

## Acceptance Criteria
- All rebase commands explicitly use `--no-autosquash` 
- Test with a stack containing `fixup! <commit-msg>` commits to verify they stay in place
- Document this behavior if needed

title: Create Story API and logger
description:
Read the parent epic spry-mbn for full context.

## Task
Create the Story API in tests/helpers/story.ts that allows tests to log narratives and command outputs.

## Interface

```
interface Story {
  begin(testName: string): void;
  narrate(text: string): void;
  log(result: CommandResult): void;
  end(): void;
  flush(): Promise<void>;
}
```

## Implementation

StoryLogger class that:
- Checks TASPR_STORY_TEST_LOGGING=1 env var to enable/disable
- Buffers entries as interleaved narrate/command entries
- On flush(), writes both .md (ANSI stripped) and .ansi (colors preserved) to test-logs/
- Uses stripAnsi regex: /\x1b\[[0-9;]*m/g

## Test ID Sanitization

The test fixtures create repos with random IDs (e.g., `humble-panda-zl3`) that appear throughout output. These must be stripped from story logs to create stable, readable documentation.

**What to strip:**
- Branch names: `feature-humble-panda-zl3` → `feature-{test-id}`
- Commit messages: `First commit [humble-panda-zl3]` → `First commit`
- Anywhere else the test ID appears in stdout/stderr

**Implementation approach:**
- `createStory()` accepts an optional `testId` parameter
- When set, the Story replaces all occurrences of the test ID with a placeholder or removes it entirely
- This happens during `log()` before buffering, so both .md and .ansi outputs are clean

**Example transformation:**
```
Stack: feature-humble-panda-zl3 (3 commits, PRs: 0/2 opened)
  ○ First grouped commit [humble-panda-zl3] [group-abc]
```
becomes:
```
Stack: feature-{id} (3 commits, PRs: 0/2 opened)
  ○ First grouped commit [group-abc]
```

## Output Format

Each story section in the markdown:
```
---

## {testName}

{narrative text}

### `{command}`

```
{stdout}
```

---
```

## Files
- tests/helpers/story.ts (new file)

## Acceptance
- Story API works when env var is set
- No-ops silently when env var is not set
- Generates readable markdown output
- Test IDs are sanitized from output when testId is provided
close_reason: Implemented Story API with test ID sanitization

title: taspr-inmem-finalize
description:
# Finalize In-Memory Operations

Implement the finalization step that updates refs and optionally resets working directory.

## Function

```typescript
async function finalizeRewrite(
  branch: string,
  oldTip: string,
  newTip: string,
  options: GitOptions = {}
): Promise<void>
```

## Algorithm

```typescript
const oldTree = await getTree(oldTip);
const newTree = await getTree(newTip);

// Update branch ref atomically with compare-and-swap
await updateRef(\`refs/heads/\${branch}\`, newTip, oldTip);

// Only reset if tree changed
// Skip for message-only or trailer-only changes
if (oldTree !== newTree) {
  await $\`git reset --hard HEAD\`;
}
```

## Key Optimization
**Tree comparison determines reset need:**
- Message-only changes: Same tree → no reset needed
- Trailer additions: Same tree → no reset needed
- Actual content changes (rebase): Different tree → reset needed

This is why in-memory is so fast for trailer operations - the working directory is never touched.

## Safety
- Uses compare-and-swap to prevent race conditions
- Creates reflog entry automatically
- If reset fails, ref is still updated (safe state)

## Tests
- Message-only change: verify no reset
- Content change: verify reset happens
- Compare-and-swap failure handling

title: Epic 8: Rebase and Conflict Handling
description:
## Goal
Handle rebasing onto updated main and conflict resolution.

## Background

### The Rebase Problem
As main advances, stacks fall behind. `taspr sync` should:
1. Detect when stack is behind origin/main
2. Rebase stack onto latest main
3. Handle conflicts gracefully
4. Update all affected PR branches

### Conflict Scenarios
1. **Automatic resolve**: Git handles the conflict automatically
2. **Manual resolve**: User must fix conflicts
3. **Stack cascade**: Rebasing bottom changes all commits above

## Workflow

### Normal Rebase
```
$ taspr sync

✓ Rebased onto origin/main (4 commits)
✓ Updated 3 PR branches
```

### Conflict
```
$ taspr sync

Rebasing onto origin/main...
✗ Conflict in src/auth.ts

  Please resolve the conflict and run:
    git add src/auth.ts
    git rebase --continue
    taspr sync
```

## Deliverable
`taspr sync` rebases if behind origin/main, with clear conflict handling.

## Dependencies
- Epic 4 must be complete (branch updates)

## Key Considerations
- Preserving Taspr-Commit-Id trailers through rebase
- Updating all affected branches after rebase
- Clear instructions for manual conflict resolution

## Priority
P2 - Robustness feature after MVP.

title: Sync command (ID injection only)
description:
## Goal
Implement the `taspr sync` command for Epic 2 scope: adding IDs to commits.

## Background
At this stage, sync does one thing: ensure all commits have Taspr-Commit-Id trailers.

Later epics will extend sync to:
- Push branches to GitHub (Epic 3)
- Update PR branches (Epic 4)
- Rebase onto main (Epic 8)

## Command Implementation

```typescript
// src/cli/commands/sync.ts
import { Command } from 'commander';

export const syncCommand = new Command('sync')
  .description('Sync stack state (add IDs, push PRs)')
  .action(async () => {
    // Check working tree
    await requireCleanWorkingTree();
    
    // Parse current stack
    const result = await parseStack();
    if (!result.ok) {
      console.error(formatValidationError(result));
      process.exit(1);
    }
    
    // Find commits without IDs
    const needsId = result.units
      .flatMap(u => u.commits)
      .filter(c => !c.trailers['Taspr-Commit-Id']);
    
    if (needsId.length === 0) {
      console.log('✓ All commits have IDs');
      return;
    }
    
    // Add IDs via rebase
    console.log(`Adding IDs to ${needsId.length} commit(s)...`);
    await injectMissingIds();
    
    console.log('✓ Added Taspr-Commit-Id to commits');
  });
```

## Output

### When IDs added
```
$ taspr sync

Adding IDs to 3 commit(s)...
✓ Added Taspr-Commit-Id to 3 commits:
  • a1b2c3d4 Add user model
  • b2c3d4e5 Add login endpoint
  • c3d4e5f6 Add validation
```

### When already up to date
```
$ taspr sync

✓ All commits have Taspr-Commit-Id
```

### When dirty
```
$ taspr sync

✗ Error: Cannot sync with uncommitted changes
  ...
```

## Integration with View
After sync, `taspr view` will show the new IDs:

```
$ taspr view

Stack: feature/auth (3 commits, 3 PRs)
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ○ Add user model
    └─ a1b2c3d4
...
```

## Testing
1. Stack with no IDs → adds IDs, reports count
2. Stack with all IDs → no-op, reports up to date
3. Dirty working tree → blocks with error
4. Integration: sync then view shows IDs

## Files to Create/Modify
- src/cli/commands/sync.ts (create)
- src/cli/index.ts (modify) - Register sync command

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Adds IDs to commits without them
- [ ] Reports how many commits were modified
- [ ] No-op if all commits have IDs
- [ ] Blocks on dirty working tree
- [ ] Has integration tests
close_reason: Implemented taspr sync command with 5 integration tests

title: Fix group integrity when fixup! commits are squashed
description:
## Problem

When a group contains a fixup! commit that gets squashed via `git rebase --autosquash`, the group loses a commit and becomes invalid (missing end marker). This causes sync to fail with a group error.

**Example scenario:**
1. Create commit A (single commit, gets PR)
2. Create `fixup! A` commit
3. Group them together (now A has Group-Start, fixup! has Group-End)
4. Run `git rebase -i --autosquash origin/main` - fixup! squashes into A
5. Group-End trailer is lost
6. `taspr sync` fails: unclosed group

## Possible Solutions

1. **All grouped commits get shared ID** - Instead of Start/End markers, all commits in a group share the same Taspr-Commit-Id. Squashing doesn't break anything since the ID persists.

2. **Custom autosquash command** - `taspr squash` that handles group integrity before/after rebase. Knows which commits are grouped and repairs trailers.

3. **Group repair on sync** - Detect orphaned Group-Start without Group-End and auto-repair (convert to single commit).

4. **Warn before breaking operation** - Detect when autosquash would break groups and warn user.

## Related Documentation

The README documents the fixup! workflow but doesn't mention this edge case. Look for marker:
`FIXUP_GROUP_DOCS` in README.md - update after resolving this issue.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Squashing fixup! commits doesn't break group integrity
- [ ] Clear error or auto-repair when group becomes invalid
- [ ] Documentation updated (remove FIXUP_GROUP_DOCS marker)
close_reason: Planning complete. Implementation plan created. Solution: Replace Taspr-Group-Start/End markers with Taspr-Group and Taspr-Group-Title on ALL commits in a group for squash resilience.

title: Trailer writing (add/update trailers)
description:
## Goal
Add the ability to write trailers to commit messages, not just read them.

## Background
Epic 1 implemented trailer reading. Now we need to write trailers:
- Add new trailers to commits
- Preserve existing trailers

## Implementation

### Using git interpret-trailers
```bash
# Add a trailer to a message
echo "commit message" | git interpret-trailers --trailer "Taspr-Commit-Id: a1b2c3d4"

# Add multiple trailers
echo "commit message" | git interpret-trailers \
  --trailer "Taspr-Commit-Id: a1b2c3d4" \
  --trailer "Taspr-Group-Start: f7e8d9c0"
```

### Function Signatures
```typescript
// src/git/trailers.ts

// Add trailers to a message string
export function addTrailers(
  message: string, 
  trailers: Record<string, string>
): Promise<string>

// Add trailers to a specific commit (amends the commit)
export async function addTrailersToCommit(
  commitHash: string, 
  trailers: Record<string, string>
): Promise<void>
```

### Amending a Commit
```typescript
async function addTrailersToCommit(hash: string, trailers: Record<string, string>) {
  // Get current message
  const message = await $`git log -1 --format=%B ${hash}`.text();
  
  // Add trailers
  const newMessage = await addTrailers(message, trailers);
  
  // Amend commit (only works for HEAD)
  // For non-HEAD commits, use rebase
  await $`git commit --amend --no-edit -m ${newMessage}`;
}
```

### Edge Cases
1. **Message already has the trailer**: Update value or skip
2. **Message has other trailers**: Preserve them
3. **Message has no body**: Still works (trailers after blank line)

## Testing
- Add trailer to message without trailers
- Add trailer to message with existing trailers
- Add multiple trailers at once
- Preserve existing unrelated trailers

## Files to Modify
- src/git/trailers.ts

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Can add single trailer to message
- [ ] Can add multiple trailers to message
- [ ] Preserves existing trailers
- [ ] Uses git interpret-trailers for correctness
- [ ] Has tests for various scenarios

title: Add GitHub API rate limiting and retry logic
description: Large stacks or repos with many PRs can hit GitHub rate limits. Operations fail without retry logic or helpful messaging. Add exponential backoff and circuit breaker for GitHub API calls.

comments.author: dondenton
comments.text:
Analysis of current GitHub API usage:

**Where calls are made:** All centralized in src/github/pr.ts (18 functions wrapping gh CLI). Good refactoring target.

**Current error handling:** Minimal - uses .nothrow() and checks stderr for specific errors. NO retry logic, NO rate limit handling, NO backoff.

**Parallel call patterns (rate limit risk):**
- sp view: 3 parallel calls per PR (checks, reviews, comments) via Promise.all
- sp land: Batch fetches merge status for all open PRs in parallel
- sp sync: Sequential PR creation but parallel cleanup

**What a fix involves:**
1. Retry wrapper (~100 lines) - exponential backoff for gh calls
2. Concurrency throttle (~80 lines) - limit to 3-5 concurrent calls  
3. Update pr.ts (~50 lines) - route calls through wrapper
4. User messaging for rate limit waits

Estimated: 400-600 lines including tests.

NOTE: The n×n call pattern in sp view is a separate issue (spry-xxx) that should be fixed first to reduce API call volume.

title: Pin git version for dev and CI testing
description:
## Context

spry requires Git 2.40+ for `git merge-tree --merge-base` support. Apple Git (even version 2.39.5) doesn't include this feature despite the version number.

Currently we have a version check test in `src/git/plumbing.test.ts` that fails with a helpful message, but we should be able to:
1. Pin a specific git version for development
2. Test against multiple git versions in CI
3. Test the "version too old" error message with an older git

## Research

**The short answer: there's no elegant solution.** Unlike Node.js (nvm) or Python (pyenv), no widely-adopted version manager exists specifically for Git.

### Why Git version management is different

Git maintains strong backward compatibility—most workflows work identically across Git 2.30 through 2.45. Few developers need version switching, which explains the sparse tooling.

### mise/asdf: technically possible, rarely practical

An asdf-git plugin exists at `gitlab.com/jcaigitlab/asdf-git`, but it's essentially abandoned (15 commits since October 2021). Every installation compiles Git from source (5-10 minutes per version), requiring gcc, make, libcurl-dev, zlib-dev, openssl-dev.

### Docker containers: the pragmatic choice

For testing against multiple Git versions, Docker provides the cleanest isolation:

```bash
docker run --rm alpine/git:v2.40.0 --version
docker run -v $(pwd):/git -w /git alpine/git:v2.40.0 status
```

For CI, a GitHub Actions matrix strategy:

```yaml
jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        git-version: ['2.38.0', '2.40.0', '2.43.0']
    container: alpine/git:v${{ matrix.git-version }}
```

### What git tool developers actually do

Most don't extensively test across Git versions. They:
- Test against the single Git version on CI runners
- Document minimum supported version requirements
- Use `git --version` parsing to conditionally enable features

## Recommendation

Use Docker containers with `alpine/git` for CI testing. For local dev, just ensure developers have Git 2.40+ installed (brew install git on macOS).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] CI tests against minimum supported git version (2.40) via Docker
- [ ] CI verifies the error message works when git is too old (2.39)
- [ ] Document Git 2.40+ requirement in README
close_reason: Implemented CI workflow with git 2.40/2.38 testing, Docker dev environment, and CLI version check

description:
Create worktree with automatic branch creation.

Usage: sp wt new <name> [options]

Behavior:
- Creates worktree in ../<repo>.worktrees/<name>/ by default
- Auto-creates branch from origin/main (or spry.tree.defaultBranch)
- Runs setup script automatically if configured

Flags:
- -e, --existing - Use existing branch instead of creating new
- -i, --initial-ref <ref> - Start new branch from specific ref
- -s, --skip-setup - Skip automatic setup script
- -d, --dir <path> - Custom worktrees directory (supports tokens)
- -o, --open <cmd> - Run command after creation (e.g., 'code')
- -O, --no-open - Skip default open command

Safety checks:
- Refuse if branch name already exists (without -e)
- Refuse if creating worktree for currently checked out branch
- Refuse if worktree directory already exists

title: taspr-inmem-core
description:
# Core In-Memory Git Primitives

Create `src/git/inmemory.ts` with low-level plumbing wrappers:

## Functions to Implement

### 1. `getTree(commit: string): Promise<string>`
Get tree SHA from a commit: `git rev-parse ${commit}^{tree}`

### 2. `getParents(commit: string): Promise<string[]>`
Get all parent SHAs: `git rev-parse ${commit}^@`

### 3. `getAuthorEnv(commit: string): Promise<Record<string, string>>`
Extract author info for preserving authorship:
- GIT_AUTHOR_NAME, GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL, GIT_AUTHOR_DATE

### 4. `getAuthorAndCommitterEnv(commit: string): Promise<Record<string, string>>`
Extract both author and committer info (for message-only and DAG-only changes):
- All author fields + GIT_COMMITTER_NAME, GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL, GIT_COMMITTER_DATE

### 5. `createCommit(tree: string, parents: string[], message: string, env: Record<string, string>): Promise<string>`
Create commit with `git commit-tree`:
```typescript
const parentFlags = parents.flatMap(p => ['-p', p]);
return $`git commit-tree ${tree} ${parentFlags} -m ${message}`.env(env).text().trim();
```

### 6. `mergeTree(base: string, ours: string, theirs: string): Promise<{ok: true, tree: string} | {ok: false}>`
Three-way merge with `git merge-tree --write-tree`:
```typescript
const result = await $`git merge-tree --write-tree --merge-base=${base} ${ours} ${theirs}`.nothrow();
if (result.exitCode !== 0) return { ok: false };
return { ok: true, tree: result.stdout.toString().trim().split('\n')[0] };
```

### 7. `updateRef(ref: string, newSha: string, oldSha?: string): Promise<void>`
Atomic ref update: `git update-ref ${ref} ${newSha} [${oldSha}]`

### 8. `checkGitVersion(): Promise<{ok: true} | {ok: false, version: string}>`
Verify Git 2.38+ for `merge-tree --write-tree` support

## Tests
- Unit tests for each function
- Test error handling (missing commits, conflicts, etc.)

title: Rebase orchestration for ID injection
description:
## Goal
Implement the rebase logic to add Taspr-Commit-Id trailers to multiple commits in a stack.

## Background
When commits don't have IDs, we need to add them via rebase. This is non-trivial because:
- Multiple commits may need IDs
- We want to preserve existing IDs
- We need to handle the rebase cleanly

## Approach: GIT_SEQUENCE_EDITOR + exec

### Option 1: Rebase with --exec
```bash
git rebase -i --exec './add-id-if-missing.sh' <merge-base>
```

The script checks each commit and adds ID only if missing:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
if ! git log -1 --format=%B | grep -q "^Taspr-Commit-Id:"; then
  # Add ID
  NEW_MSG=$(git log -1 --format=%B | git interpret-trailers --trailer "Taspr-Commit-Id: $(openssl rand -hex 4)")
  git commit --amend --no-edit -m "$NEW_MSG"
fi
```

### Option 2: Programmatic Rebase
More control but more complex:
1. Generate todo list manually
2. For each commit needing ID, mark as "edit"
3. Use GIT_SEQUENCE_EDITOR to inject todo
4. Handle each "edit" stop to add trailer

### Recommended: Option 1
Simpler and handles edge cases well. The --exec runs after each pick.

## Implementation

```typescript
// src/core/sync.ts

async function injectMissingIds(): Promise<{ modifiedCommits: string[] }> {
  const commits = await getStackCommits();
  
  // Find commits without IDs
  const needsId = commits.filter(c => !c.trailers['Taspr-Commit-Id']);
  
  if (needsId.length === 0) {
    return { modifiedCommits: [] };
  }
  
  // Create temp script for --exec
  const script = createIdInjectionScript();
  await writeFile(scriptPath, script);
  await chmod(scriptPath, '755');
  
  // Run rebase
  const mergeBase = await getMergeBase();
  await $`git rebase -i --exec ${scriptPath} ${mergeBase}`;
  
  // Clean up
  await unlink(scriptPath);
  
  return { modifiedCommits: needsId.map(c => c.hash) };
}
```

## Challenges

### Non-interactive rebase
We want this to run without user intervention. Using `--exec` with a script achieves this—no editor opens.

### Preserving existing IDs
The script only adds ID if missing, so existing IDs are preserved.

### Rebase conflicts
If there's a conflict, the rebase stops. This is handled by a separate task in Epic 8.

## Testing
1. Stack with no IDs → all get IDs
2. Stack with some IDs → only missing ones get IDs
3. Stack with all IDs → no rebase happens (no-op)
4. Verify IDs survive subsequent rebases

## Files to Create/Modify
- src/core/sync.ts (create)
- src/git/rebase.ts (create) - Rebase utilities

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Adds IDs to commits missing them
- [ ] Preserves existing IDs
- [ ] No-op if all commits have IDs
- [ ] Works without user interaction
- [ ] Has integration tests with real git repos

title: taspr-inmem
description:
# Migrate to In-Memory Git Operations

## Problem
Traditional `git rebase` touches the working directory at each commit during replay, causing "untracked working tree files would be overwritten" errors when files were tracked in history, later removed and gitignored, then recreated as untracked files.

## Solution
Replace all `git rebase -i` operations with in-memory git plumbing commands:
- `git commit-tree` for creating new commits
- `git merge-tree --write-tree` for computing merged trees (Git 2.38+)
- `git update-ref` for moving branch pointers atomically

## Benefits
- Working directory never touched during intermediate states
- 10-150x performance improvement
- Conflict detection without modification
- Single reset at end when tree actually changes

## Scope
Migrate these operations:
1. **Message rewrites** - Use `git commit-tree` with same tree, new message
2. **Trailer injection** - Rewrite message with `git interpret-trailers`, commit via `commit-tree`
3. **Group operations** - All trailer modifications use in-memory approach
4. **Rebase onto main** - Use `git merge-tree --write-tree` for cherry-pick sequence

## Files to Migrate
- `src/git/rebase.ts` - `injectMissingIds`, `rebaseOntoMain`
- `src/git/group-rebase.ts` - All functions using `runInteractiveRebase`

## Requirements
- Git 2.38+ (for `git merge-tree --write-tree`)
- Add version check with helpful error message
close_reason: Epic complete\! All 8 tasks finished. Migrated from git rebase -i with exec commands to in-memory git operations using plumbing commands. This provides 10-150x performance improvement for message-only operations and eliminates 'untracked working tree files would be overwritten' errors.

title: Implement sp wt setup command
description:
Setup script initialization and execution.

Usage:
- sp wt setup --init [path] - Create template setup script
- sp wt setup [branch-name] - Run setup for worktree

--init mode:
- Creates template bash script at path (default: setup-worktree.sh)
- Sets spry.tree.setupScript config
- Template receives $1=repo_root, $2=worktree_root
- Non-.sh extensions get modified template with manual instructions

Run mode:
- If branch provided: find worktree for that branch, run setup
- If no branch: must be inside linked worktree, run setup for current
- Expand tokens in setupScript path before execution

Token expansion in paths:
- <repo_root> - absolute path to main repository
- <repo_name> - basename of repository
- <worktree_root> - absolute path to target worktree

Reference: ~/dotfiles/bin/git-ht cmd_setup()

title: Epic 5: Land PRs (Merge to Main)
description:
## Goal
Merge the bottom PR to main, completing the PR lifecycle.

## Background

### Landing Strategy
When a PR is ready (approved, CI passing), land it by merging to main. The merge strategy depends on repo settings:
- Fast-forward (if possible)
- Squash merge
- Merge commit

taspr uses GitHub's merge API, which respects repo settings.

### Stack Behavior After Landing
When the bottom PR merges:
1. GitHub automatically retargets child PRs to new base (main)
2. No action needed from taspr for retargeting
3. Next `taspr sync` will clean up merged PR from local tracking

### What "Ready" Means
A PR is ready to land when:
- Approved by required reviewers
- CI checks passing
- No merge conflicts

## Workflow

1. User runs `taspr land`
2. Find bottom unmerged PR
3. Check if it's ready (API call)
4. Merge via GitHub API
5. Report success

## Deliverable
Running `taspr land` merges the bottom PR. GitHub handles retargeting child PRs.

## Dependencies
- Epic 4 must be complete (PRs exist and are synced)

## Commands

### taspr land
Merge the bottom ready PR:
```
$ taspr land

Merging PR #142 (Add user model)...
✓ Merged PR #142 to main
```

### taspr land --all
Merge all consecutive ready PRs:
```
$ taspr land --all

Merging PR #142 (Add user model)...
✓ Merged PR #142 to main

Merging PR #143 (Add auth)...
✓ Merged PR #143 to main

Stopping at PR #144 (not ready: CI failing)
```

## Testable Outcome
Landing a PR merges it to main. GitHub retargets child PRs automatically.
close_reason: All child tasks completed: PR merge API, land command, land --all flag, and merged PR detection/cleanup are all implemented.

title: Flag test failures in story output

- Use a different file naming convention for failed tests
- Add a summary section at the top listing any failures

The Story API will need a way to capture test failure state, possibly via a `story.fail(reason)` method or by checking test results in `afterAll`.

title: PR Content Manipulation Epic
description:
## Overview

Add comprehensive PR body management to Spry:
- Auto-generate PR bodies from commit messages
- Support user-editable content between granular Spry markers
- Optional stack links showing all PRs in order (using GitHub native PR refs)
- Alpha warning footer
- Update bodies on sync when content changes
- PR template support with configurable placement

## PR Body Structure

```
<!-- spry:info - Your edits outside of spry markers will be preserved on sync. -->
[User prepended content]
<!-- spry:body:begin -->
[Commit message / group list]
<!-- spry:body:end -->
[PR template + user content]
<!-- spry:stack-links:begin -->
**Stack** (oldest → newest, targeting `main`):
- #123
- #124 ← this PR
- #125
<!-- spry:stack-links:end -->
[User content]
<!-- spry:footer:begin -->
<sub>Alpha warning</sub>
<!-- spry:footer:end -->
[User appended content]
```

## Key Features
- Hidden info comment explains edit preservation
- Native GitHub PR references for stack links
- Target branch shown in stack links header
- Two-pass creation for stack links (create PRs → update with links)
- Configurable settings with 3-level override (global → repo → per-stack)

## Settings
| Setting | Default | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| showStackLinks | true | Show/hide stack links |
| includePrTemplate | true | Include PR template |
| prTemplateLocation | afterBody | Where to place template |

## Plan File
See: .claude/plans/linked-sleeping-whale.md

description:
## Goal
Create `src/github/pr-body.ts` with PR body generation and parsing utilities.

## Constants
```typescript
const MARKERS = {
  INFO: "<!-- spry:info - Your edits outside of spry markers will be preserved on sync. -->",
  BODY_BEGIN: "<!-- spry:body:begin -->",
  BODY_END: "<!-- spry:body:end -->",
  STACK_LINKS_BEGIN: "<!-- spry:stack-links:begin -->",
  STACK_LINKS_END: "<!-- spry:stack-links:end -->",
  FOOTER_BEGIN: "<!-- spry:footer:begin -->",
  FOOTER_END: "<!-- spry:footer:end -->",
};

const ALPHA_WARNING = `<sub>Created with [Spry](https://github.com/happycollision/spry) (alpha). Do not manually merge stacked PRs.</sub>`;
```

## Key Types
- `PRBodyParts` - parsed sections from existing body
- `StackPRInfo` - PR number/title for stack links
- `GenerateInitialPRBodyOptions` - options for new PR creation
- `UpdatePRBodyOptions` - options for updating existing PR

## Functions
- `stripTrailersFromBody(body)` - Remove git trailers
- `findPRTemplate()` - Find PR template in standard locations
- `generateBodyContent(options)` - Commit message content
- `generateStackLinksContent(stackPRs, currentIndex, targetBranch)` - Stack links using native GH refs (#123)
- `generateFooterContent()` - Alpha warning
- `generateInitialPRBody(options)` - Full body for new PR (includes info comment, template placement)
- `generateUpdatedPRBody(options)` - Updated body preserving user content in all gaps
- `parsePRBody(body)` - Extract PRBodyParts
- `calculateContentHash(bodyContent, stackLinksContent)` - For change detection

## Stack Links Format
```markdown
**Stack** (oldest → newest, targeting `main`):
- #123
- #124 ← this PR
- #125
```
Uses GitHub native PR references - GitHub auto-renders title/status/icon.

## Info Comment
Hidden comment at top of freshly opened PRs:
`<!-- spry:info - Your edits outside of spry markers will be preserved on sync. -->`

## Tests
- Trailer stripping
- PR template discovery
- Body generation (single + groups)
- Stack links formatting
- User content preservation
- Hash calculation

title: Trailer parsing
description:
## Goal
Extract taspr-specific trailers from commit messages.

## Background
Git trailers are key-value pairs at the end of commit messages, following a specific format:
```
feat: add user authentication

Implements login flow with session management.

Taspr-Commit-Id: a1b2c3d4
Taspr-Group-Start: f7e8d9c0
Taspr-Group-Title: Authentication feature
```

taspr uses these trailers:
- `Taspr-Commit-Id`: 8 hex chars, unique ID for each commit (survives rebases)
- `Taspr-Group-Start`: 8 hex chars, marks the first commit of a group
- `Taspr-Group-Title`: String, PR title for the group
- `Taspr-Group-End`: 8 hex chars (same as Group-Start), marks the last commit of a group

## Implementation

### Using git interpret-trailers
```bash
git interpret-trailers --parse <<< "commit message body"
```

This outputs:
```
Taspr-Commit-Id: a1b2c3d4
Taspr-Group-Start: f7e8d9c0
```

### Function Signatures
```typescript
interface CommitTrailers {
  'Taspr-Commit-Id'?: string;
  'Taspr-Group-Start'?: string;
  'Taspr-Group-Title'?: string;
  'Taspr-Group-End'?: string;
  [key: string]: string | undefined;  // Allow other trailers
}

async function parseTrailers(commitBody: string): Promise<CommitTrailers>

// Convenience function to get trailers for a commit
async function getCommitTrailers(commitHash: string): Promise<CommitTrailers>
```

### Edge Cases
1. **No trailers**: Return empty object
2. **Non-taspr trailers**: Include them (e.g., Co-authored-by)
3. **Malformed trailers**: Let git interpret-trailers handle
4. **Multiple values for same key**: Use last value (git's behavior)

## Testing Strategy
Test with various commit message formats:
1. No trailers
2. Single trailer
3. Multiple trailers
4. Mixed taspr and other trailers
5. Trailers with special characters in values

## Files to Create/Modify
- src/git/trailers.ts (create)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Correctly parses all taspr trailer types
- [ ] Handles commits without trailers
- [ ] Uses git interpret-trailers for correct parsing
- [ ] Has unit tests for various message formats
close_reason: Implemented parseTrailers() and getCommitTrailers() in src/git/trailers.ts with 13 passing tests

title: Rework taspr view to handle groups
description:
## Goal
Update `taspr view` to properly display groups in the stack visualization.

## Current Behavior
The `taspr view` command shows individual commits but doesn't visually distinguish grouped commits or show group boundaries.

## Desired Behavior
- Show group boundaries clearly in the stack view
- Display group title/name for grouped commits
- Indicate which commits belong to which group
- Show group status (e.g., if group has a PR)
- Consider collapsible group view for large groups

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Groups are visually distinguishable from individual commits
- [ ] Group title is displayed
- [ ] Group boundaries are clear (start/end)
- [ ] Works with both single-commit and multi-commit groups
- [ ] Maintains readability for mixed stacks (groups + singles)

description:
Currently spry claims to support custom default branches (e.g., 'master', 'development') and custom remote names (e.g., 'github', 'upstream'), but we haven't verified this with tests.

We need to:
1. Add test scenarios for repositories with default branches other than 'main' (e.g., 'master', 'development', 'develop')
2. Add test scenarios for repositories with remote names other than 'origin' (e.g., 'github', 'upstream', 'fork')
3. Verify all spry commands work correctly with these variations
4. Fix any issues discovered during testing

This will ensure spry truly supports diverse git configurations as documented.

title: Add taspr group --fix command to repair invalid group trailers
description:
After resolving rebase conflicts (especially when commits at group boundaries are removed), group trailers can become invalid. Add a --fix flag to taspr group that:

1. Detects invalid group trailer states:
   - Taspr-Group-Start without matching Taspr-Group-End
   - Taspr-Group-End without matching Taspr-Group-Start
   - Orphaned Taspr-Group-Title trailers
   
2. Offers repair options:
   - Remove all group trailers from affected commits (reset to ungrouped)
   - Attempt to repair by finding the closest valid group boundary
   
3. Can run non-interactively with --fix=remove or --fix=repair

This helps users recover after conflict resolution without needing to manually edit commit messages.
close_reason: Implemented taspr group --fix command: detects invalid groups (unclosed, overlapping, orphan ends) and removes all group trailers to repair. Added 5 integration tests.

title: Epic 7: Group Management
description:
## Goal
Interactive creation and management of commit groups (multi-commit PRs) via a unified TUI.

## Background

### Groups
By default, each commit becomes its own PR. Groups let you bundle multiple commits into a single PR.

Groups are defined by trailers:
- `Taspr-Group-Start: <group-id>` on first commit
- `Taspr-Group-Title: <title>` on first commit (PR title)
- `Taspr-Group-End: <group-id>` on last commit

### Use Cases
- Related changes that should be reviewed together
- Work-in-progress commits that will be squashed
- Logical feature that spans multiple commits

## Revised Plan (January 2026)

The original plan for separate `group create/edit/dissolve` commands has been replaced with a **unified interactive TUI**.

### taspr group
Launches a full-screen terminal UI that allows:
- **Reordering commits** via move mode (Space to enter, Up/Down to swap)
- **Assigning groups** via Left/Right arrows (cycles through A, B, C, ... Z, ungrouped)
- **Conflict prediction** warns when moves would cause merge conflicts
- **Single-commit groups** are allowed

Controls:
- Up/Down: navigate (normal) / swap position (move mode)
- Space: toggle move mode
- Left/Right: cycle group assignment
- Enter: confirm → name groups → rebase
- Esc: cancel

### taspr group dissolve
Simple command to remove a group's trailers and break it back into individual commits.

## Deliverables
1. **Unified group TUI** (spry-6q3) - main interactive interface
2. **Conflict prediction** (spry-0bl) - hybrid file-overlap + git merge-tree
3. **Group rebase logic** (spry-fx7) - reordering and trailer injection
4. **Group dissolve** (spry-my0) - remove group trailers
5. **Sync validation** (spry-gx3) - block sync on invalid groups

## Dependencies
- Epic 2 must be complete (commits have IDs)

## Validation
Groups must not overlap and must be closed (Start has matching End).

## Priority
P2 - Enhancement after basic workflow is complete.
notes:
## Scenario-Based Testing Workflow

All tasks in this epic should follow this testing workflow:

### After Implementation
1. Run `bun run build` to build the CLI
2. Run `bun test --no-coverage` to verify all tests pass
3. Tell the user which scenario to run for manual testing

### Available Scenarios
Run `bun run scenario --help` or `bun run scenario` for interactive selection:
- **emptyStack** - Just main branch, no feature work
- **singleCommit** - One commit on feature branch (no ID)
- **multiCommitStack** - 3 commits stacked on feature branch
- **divergedMain** - Feature branch + upstream changes (needs rebase)
- **withTasprIds** - 2 commits with Taspr-Commit-Id trailers
- **mixedTrailerStack** - Some commits have IDs, some don't
- **conflictScenario** - Setup that will conflict on rebase
- **multipleBranches** - Two independent feature branches

### New Scenarios Needed for Group Management
Add these to `src/scenario/definitions.ts` as tasks are implemented:
- **withGroups** - Stack with existing group trailers (Group-Start/End/Title)
- **unclosedGroup** - Stack with Group-Start but no Group-End
- **overlappingGroups** - Stack with overlapping group trailers

### Scenario Definition Location
`src/scenario/definitions.ts` - add new scenarios following the existing pattern

title: taspr-inmem-rewrite
description:
# In-Memory Message Rewrite

Implement message rewriting without touching the working directory.

## Function

```typescript
async function rewriteCommitMessage(
  commit: string,
  newMessage: string,
  options: GitOptions = {}
): Promise<string>
```

## Algorithm

1. Get tree from original commit (unchanged)
2. Get parents from original commit (unchanged)
3. Get author AND committer env (preserve both for message-only changes)
4. Create new commit with `git commit-tree`
5. Return new commit SHA

## Key Points
- Tree is reused (no content change)
- Both author and committer dates preserved (message-only = no timestamp update)
- No working directory access at all

## Usage Locations
- Add/remove/modify trailers (Taspr-Commit-Id, Taspr-Group)
- All operations in `group-rebase.ts` that only change messages

## Tests
- Verify tree SHA unchanged
- Verify author/committer info preserved
- Verify message correctly modified

title: Epic 2: Initialize Commits for Tracking
description:
## Goal
Add `Taspr-Commit-Id` trailers to commits that don't have them, enabling stable tracking across rebases.

## Background

### The Tracking Problem
Git commit hashes change whenever a commit is rebased, amended, or cherry-picked. taspr needs a stable identifier that survives these operations.

### The Solution: Commit ID Trailers
Every commit gets a `Taspr-Commit-Id` trailer—an 8-character hex string that becomes part of the commit message:

```
feat: add user authentication

Implements login flow.

Taspr-Commit-Id: a1b2c3d4
```

This ID:
- Survives rebases (message is preserved)
- Links commits to branches and PRs
- Enables tracking across history rewrites

### ID Format
- 8 hexadecimal characters (32 bits)
- Generated via crypto.randomBytes(4).toString('hex')
- Example: `a1b2c3d4`, `f7e8d9c0`

With 8 hex chars, collision probability is negligible for a typical stack (< 50 commits). Once PRs merge, their commits leave the active tracking set.

## Workflow

1. User has commits without IDs
2. Run `taspr sync`
3. taspr identifies commits missing `Taspr-Commit-Id`
4. taspr performs a rebase to add trailers to those commits
5. Existing IDs are preserved; only missing ones are added

## Implementation Approach

### Interactive Rebase with exec
```bash
git rebase -i --exec 'git commit --amend --no-edit -m "$(git log -1 --format=%B | git interpret-trailers --trailer "Taspr-Commit-Id: $(openssl rand -hex 4)")"' <merge-base>
```

Or, more cleanly with a custom script that:
1. Checks if commit already has Taspr-Commit-Id
2. Only adds if missing
3. Preserves all existing trailers

### Sequence
1. Check for dirty working tree (block if dirty)
2. Find commits without Taspr-Commit-Id
3. If none, no-op
4. Rebase from merge-base, adding IDs to commits that need them
5. Report which commits got IDs

## Deliverable
Running `taspr sync` adds stable IDs to all commits in the stack via rebase, making them trackable.

## Dependencies
- Epic 1 must be complete (stack reading, trailer parsing)

## Files to Create/Modify
- src/core/sync.ts (create) - Sync orchestration
- src/git/trailers.ts (modify) - Add trailer writing
- src/cli/commands/sync.ts (create) - Sync command

## Testable Outcome
- New commits get IDs
- Existing IDs are preserved through rebase
- Dirty working tree blocks sync with clear message
close_reason: All 5 child tasks complete. taspr sync now adds Taspr-Commit-Id trailers to commits.

title: Integration: Make merge commits real in git
'sp sync' should fast-forward local main if behind origin
description:
When running 'sp sync', we should automatically fast-forward the user's local main branch (or equivalent default branch) if it is behind the remote version.

We need to:
1. Check if local main is behind origin/main
2. If it can be fast-forwarded (no divergence), automatically update it
3. Print a clear warning if the local main cannot be fast-forwarded (e.g., has local commits, diverged history)
4. Handle the user's configured default branch name (main, master, development, etc.)
5. Handle the user's configured remote name (origin, upstream, etc.)

This keeps the local main branch in sync with the remote automatically, making 'sp sync' more convenient and reducing the chance of stale local branches.

title: Flaky test: land retargets next PR to main
description:
## Failed Test
```
GitHub Integration: land > retargets next PR to main after landing
```

## How to Run
```bash
GITHUB_INTEGRATION_TESTS=1 bun test tests/integration/land.test.ts -t 'retargets next PR'
```

## Error
```
Expected to contain: "✓ Merged PR #92 to main"
Received: "Merging PR #90 (retarget 1 [cosmic-heron-ter])...\n✓ Merged PR #90 to main\n..."
```

## Analysis
The test creates 3 commits, syncs to create 3 PRs (#90, #91, #92), then lands the first one.

**Root cause suspicion:** The test uses `repo.findPRs(repo.uniqueId)` and assumes `prs[0]` is the first/bottom PR. But `findPRs` likely returns PRs in creation order (newest first) or some other order, not stack order.

So `firstPr` gets #92 (the last created), but `sp land` correctly merges #90 (the actual bottom of stack).

**Fix approach:** Sort PRs by number ascending before assigning to firstPr/secondPr/thirdPr, or use a more explicit lookup.

## Location
tests/integration/land.test.ts:91

title: Interactive group repair mode for --fix
description:
The current `taspr group --fix` just removes all group trailers, which is a destructive 'nuclear option'. Instead, it should launch an interactive TUI that:

1. Shows the detected error (unclosed group, overlapping groups, orphan end)
2. Visualizes the problematic commits and their current group trailers
3. Offers repair options:
   - For unclosed groups: select which commit should be the group end
   - For overlapping groups: choose which group to keep, or adjust boundaries
   - For orphan ends: select which commit should be the group start, or remove the orphan
4. Allows dissolving as a last resort (current behavior)

The TUI should reuse the existing group-editor infrastructure where possible.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Interactive mode shows the specific error clearly
- [ ] User can select repair action (not just dissolve)
- [ ] For unclosed groups: can pick the end commit
- [ ] For overlapping groups: can adjust which commits belong to which group
- [ ] For orphan ends: can pick the start commit or remove
- [ ] Non-interactive fallback with `--fix=dissolve` preserves current behavior

title: Git operations: Preserve merge commits in rebase operations
VCR-style testing for GitHub API calls
description:
## Goal
Implement VCR-style (record/replay) testing infrastructure for testing code that calls the GitHub API via the `gh` CLI.

## Background
The sync command with `--open` flag makes calls to:
- `gh api user --jq .login` (get username)
- `gh pr list --head <branch>` (find existing PRs)  
- `gh pr create` (create new PRs)

These calls can't be tested in CI without real GitHub auth. VCR-style testing records real CLI outputs and replays them in tests.

## Approach: Shell Executor Abstraction

After researching HTTP-level options (nock, MSW), we determined CLI-level recording is the right approach because:
- The code shells out to `gh` CLI, not HTTP directly
- HTTP mocking would require proxying subprocess traffic (complex)
- CLI-level captures real responses with simpler implementation
- No battle-tested CLI VCR library exists, so we build our own

## Architecture

### Key Interfaces

```typescript
interface ShellResult {
  stdout: Buffer;
  stderr: Buffer;
  exitCode: number;
}

interface ShellExecutor {
  exec(command: string, args: string[]): Promise<ShellResult>;
}

interface Cassette {
  name: string;
  entries: FixtureEntry[];  // {command, args, stdout, stderr, exitCode}
  recordedAt: string;
}
```

### Three Executor Implementations

1. **RealExecutor** - Runs actual commands via Bun's `$`
2. **RecordingExecutor** - Wraps RealExecutor, saves responses to cassette file
3. **ReplayExecutor** - Reads from cassette file, matches command+args exactly

### Cassette Format (JSON)
```json
{
  "name": "github-auth",
  "entries": [
    {
      "command": "gh",
      "args": ["api", "user", "--jq", ".login"],
      "stdout": "base64-encoded-output",
      "stderr": "",
      "exitCode": 0
    }
  ],
  "recordedAt": "2024-12-27T20:00:00.000Z"
}
```

## File Structure

```
src/testing/vcr/
  types.ts          # ShellResult, ShellExecutor, Cassette interfaces
  executor.ts       # RealExecutor, RecordingExecutor, ReplayExecutor
  index.ts          # Public exports
  executor.test.ts  # Self-tests using echo/sh commands (not gh!)

tests/
  fixtures/cassettes/     # JSON cassette files
  helpers/vcr-fixture.ts  # withVCR() test helper
```

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Core VCR Library (test with echo/sh, not gh)
1. Create `src/testing/vcr/types.ts` - interfaces
2. Create `src/testing/vcr/executor.ts` - three executor implementations
3. Create `src/testing/vcr/index.ts` - exports
4. Create `src/testing/vcr/executor.test.ts` - self-tests

### Phase 2: Test Helper
1. Create `tests/fixtures/cassettes/` directory
2. Create `tests/helpers/vcr-fixture.ts` with `withVCR()` helper

### Phase 3: Refactor GitHub Module
Add optional `executor` parameter (backward compatible):
1. `src/github/api.ts` - getGitHubUsername(), getDefaultBranch()
2. `src/github/pr.ts` - findPRByBranch(), createPR()
3. `src/github/branches.ts` - getBranchNameConfig()

### Phase 4: Record Fixtures & Add Tests
1. Run in record mode against real GitHub to create cassettes
2. Add VCR tests for `--open` flag in `sync.test.ts`

## Files to Modify
- src/github/api.ts - add executor injection
- src/github/pr.ts - add executor injection
- src/github/branches.ts - add executor injection
- src/cli/commands/sync.test.ts - add --open tests (has TODO comment)

## Files to Create
- src/testing/vcr/types.ts
- src/testing/vcr/executor.ts
- src/testing/vcr/index.ts
- src/testing/vcr/executor.test.ts
- tests/helpers/vcr-fixture.ts
- tests/fixtures/cassettes/*.json

## Testing Strategy for VCR Library Itself
The VCR library must be tested without circular dependencies. Use predictable shell commands:
- `echo hello` → captures stdout
- `sh -c 'exit 42'` → captures exit codes
- `sh -c 'echo error >&2'` → captures stderr

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] VCR library has passing self-tests (executor.test.ts)
- [ ] GitHub functions accept optional executor parameter
- [ ] Recording mode captures real gh responses to cassettes
- [ ] Replay mode uses recorded fixtures (no network)
- [ ] sync --open has comprehensive tests using VCR

title: Add --apply flag for sync --open
description:
Add a non-interactive way to specify which commits/groups should have PRs opened.

## Syntax
```bash
taspr sync --open --apply='["id1", "id2"]'
```

## Identifier Resolution
Each identifier in the array can be:
- Git commit hash (full or partial)
- Taspr-Commit-Id (the 8-char ID in trailers)
- Group ID

## Behavior
- Only opens PRs for the specified commits/groups
- All commits still get branches pushed (for stacking)
- Validates all identifiers exist before starting
- Skips identifiers that already have PRs (no error)

## Format
Simple JSON array of identifiers:
```json
["abc123", "def456", "group-id"]
```

## Error Cases
- Invalid JSON: "Error: Invalid --apply format. Expected JSON array of identifiers."
- Ambiguous identifier: "Error: 'abc' matches multiple commits. Please provide more characters."
- Not found: "Error: No commit or group matching 'xyz' found in stack"

## Implementation Notes
- This provides the data interface for the interactive mode (-i)
- Interactive mode will build this array through TUI selection
- Reuse identifier resolution logic from --up-to implementation
close_reason: Implemented --apply flag for sync --open. Added identifier module for resolving commits/groups by various identifiers.

Implement squash-resilient group markers
description:
## Summary

Replace `Taspr-Group-Start`/`Taspr-Group-End` markers with redundant markers on ALL commits in a group for squash resilience.

## Solution

Every commit in a group gets **both**:
- `Taspr-Group: <groupId>`
- `Taspr-Group-Title: <title>`

No Start/End markers needed. Group ends when we encounter a commit without the same group marker.

**Why this works:**
- Squashing any commits leaves remaining commits with their group trailers
- Title on every commit means no fallback logic needed
- Detection is simpler: contiguous commits with same `Taspr-Group` = one group

## Files to Modify

1. **src/git/trailers.ts** - Add `Taspr-Group`, remove `Taspr-Group-Start`/`End`
2. **src/types.ts** - Add `split-group` and `inconsistent-group-title` errors, remove old errors
3. **src/core/stack.ts** - Rewrite detection: group = contiguous commits with same `Taspr-Group`
4. **src/git/group-rebase.ts** - Add both trailers to ALL commits, remove old helpers
5. **src/cli/commands/group.ts** - Update `--fix` for new error types
6. **src/tui/group-editor.ts** - Detect groups by `Taspr-Group` trailer
7. **src/cli/output.ts** - Error messages for new types
8. **Tests** - Update all group-related tests
9. **README.md** - Update docs, remove FIXUP_GROUP_DOCS marker

## Implementation Order

1. trailers.ts - Add/remove trailer types
2. types.ts - Add new error types
3. stack.ts - Update detection
4. group-rebase.ts - Update group creation
5. group.ts + group-editor.ts - Update CLI/TUI
6. output.ts - Update error messages
7. Tests
8. README.md

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Squashing fixup! commits doesn't break group integrity
- [ ] Clear error for split groups (non-contiguous commits with same ID)
- [ ] Clear error for inconsistent group titles
- [ ] `taspr group --fix` can repair split groups and inconsistent titles
- [ ] Documentation updated (remove FIXUP_GROUP_DOCS marker)

Full plan: ~/.claude/plans/binary-mapping-haven.md
close_reason: Implemented squash-resilient group markers. Replaced Taspr-Group-Start/End with Taspr-Group+Taspr-Group-Title on ALL commits in a group. Updated all related code, tests, and scenarios.

title: Create Story API and logger
description:
Read the parent epic spry-ww0 for full context.

## Task
Create the Story API in tests/helpers/story.ts that allows tests to log narratives and command outputs.

## Interface

```
interface Story {
  begin(testName: string): void;
  narrate(text: string): void;
  log(result: CommandResult): void;
  end(): void;
  flush(): Promise<void>;
}
```

## Implementation

StoryLogger class that:
- Checks TASPR_STORY_TEST_LOGGING=1 env var to enable/disable
- Buffers entries as interleaved narrate/command entries
- On flush(), writes both .md (ANSI stripped) and .ansi (colors preserved) to test-logs/
- Uses stripAnsi regex: /\x1b\[[0-9;]*m/g

## Test ID Sanitization

The test fixtures create repos with random IDs (e.g., `humble-panda-zl3`) that appear throughout output. These must be stripped from story logs to create stable, readable documentation.

**What to strip:**
- Branch names: `feature-humble-panda-zl3` → `feature-{test-id}`
- Commit messages: `First commit [humble-panda-zl3]` → `First commit`
- Anywhere else the test ID appears in stdout/stderr

**Implementation approach:**
- `createStory()` accepts an optional `testId` parameter
- When set, the Story replaces all occurrences of the test ID with a placeholder or removes it entirely
- This happens during `log()` before buffering, so both .md and .ansi outputs are clean

**Example transformation:**
```
Stack: feature-humble-panda-zl3 (3 commits, PRs: 0/2 opened)
  ○ First grouped commit [humble-panda-zl3] [group-abc]
```
becomes:
```
Stack: feature-{id} (3 commits, PRs: 0/2 opened)
  ○ First grouped commit [group-abc]
```

## Output Format

Each story section in the markdown:
```
---

## {testName}

{narrative text}

### `{command}`

```
{stdout}
```

---
```

## Files
- tests/helpers/story.ts (new file)

## Acceptance
- Story API works when env var is set
- No-ops silently when env var is not set
- Generates readable markdown output
- Test IDs are sanitized from output when testId is provided
close_reason: Implemented Story API with test ID sanitization

title: taspr-inmem
description:
# Migrate to In-Memory Git Operations

## Problem
Traditional `git rebase` touches the working directory at each commit during replay, causing "untracked working tree files would be overwritten" errors when files were tracked in history, later removed and gitignored, then recreated as untracked files.

## Solution
Replace all `git rebase -i` operations with in-memory git plumbing commands:
- `git commit-tree` for creating new commits
- `git merge-tree --write-tree` for computing merged trees (Git 2.38+)
- `git update-ref` for moving branch pointers atomically

## Benefits
- Working directory never touched during intermediate states
- 10-150x performance improvement
- Conflict detection without modification
- Single reset at end when tree actually changes

## Scope
Migrate these operations:
1. **Message rewrites** - Use `git commit-tree` with same tree, new message
2. **Trailer injection** - Rewrite message with `git interpret-trailers`, commit via `commit-tree`
3. **Group operations** - All trailer modifications use in-memory approach
4. **Rebase onto main** - Use `git merge-tree --write-tree` for cherry-pick sequence

## Files to Migrate
- `src/git/rebase.ts` - `injectMissingIds`, `rebaseOntoMain`
- `src/git/group-rebase.ts` - All functions using `runInteractiveRebase`

## Requirements
- Git 2.38+ (for `git merge-tree --write-tree`)
- Add version check with helpful error message
close_reason: Epic complete\! All 8 tasks finished. Migrated from git rebase -i with exec commands to in-memory git operations using plumbing commands. This provides 10-150x performance improvement for message-only operations and eliminates 'untracked working tree files would be overwritten' errors.

title: Pin push lease: --force-with-lease=<branch>:<sha> --force-if-includes (so checkSync's fetch can't blind the guard)
description:
Task 5 (extract checkSync from syncCommand on feat/sync-check-split) mandates that checkSync do a real 'git fetch <remote>' before the push phase, so refs/remotes/origin/* is fresh for analyzeStack. But syncCommand's push phase (pushExistingBranches -> pushBranch in src/gh/push.ts) uses BARE '--force-with-lease' (no --force-if-includes), whose lease baseline is the remote-tracking ref refs/remotes/origin/<branch>. Fetching immediately before pushing refreshes that ref to the current (possibly diverged) remote value, so the lease can no longer detect a concurrent force-push -> the push clobbers the other actor's commit instead of being rejected as stale-ref.

Concretely this breaks the existing test tests/commands/sync.test.ts 'syncCommand bare > stale-ref push prints warning and exits 1' (line ~405): it diverges origin's ref behind the local clone's back and expects a stale-ref rejection + exit 1. With checkSync's fetch, the push now succeeds and sync exits 0 (test sees exitCode -1 = process.exit never called). 33 pass / 1 fail; tsc 0 errors; oxlint 0 errors (10 pre-existing unbound-method warnings only).

This is a genuine safety regression from the mandated fetch, not a test artifact. Options to weigh (do NOT pick blindly): (a) switch pushBranch to 'git push --force-with-lease=<ref>:<expected-sha> --force-if-includes' so the lease is pinned to the pre-fetch reconciled sha rather than the freshly-fetched tracking ref; (b) capture the remote-tracking sha BEFORE checkSync's fetch and thread it into the lease; (c) narrow checkSync's fetch to only the refs analyzeStack needs (trunk + spry/prs + groups) and NOT the spry unit branches, so unit-branch tracking refs stay stale for the lease. All three change behavior beyond Task 5's stated scope, so escalated rather than guessed. File: src/commands/sync.ts (checkSync added, fetchRemote call), src/gh/push.ts (bare --force-with-lease).
close_reason: Done: pushBranch pins --force-with-lease to pre-fetch remote tip; stale-ref guard survives checkSync's fetch; commit ee3bcca

title: Review ramifications of pinned force-with-lease across all push paths
description:
Follow-up requested by Don (2026-07-12). We changed pushBranch (src/gh/push.ts) from bare '--force-with-lease' to a pinned '--force-with-lease=<branch>:<expected-sha> --force-if-includes' as part of the sync check read/write split (see spry-s2tx and epic spry-sync-check-split-mfw), because checkSync's 'git fetch' refreshes refs/remotes/origin/* and would otherwise blind the bare lease, letting sync clobber a concurrent force-push.

This bead is a deliberate later-date audit of ALL ramifications of that change, NOT part of the split's implementation. Review at minimum:
- Every caller of pushBranch (sync push phase, sync --open, sync --all per-branch, land's ff-push of trunk, any others) - does each pass a correct 'expected remote sha' baseline? What is the right baseline for the ff-push of trunk (land uses forceWithLease:false today - confirm that's still correct and a pinned lease isn't silently required there)?
- First-publish case: a brand-new branch has no remote ref - the pinned lease must degrade to 'create only if absent'. Confirm this doesn't regress first-time 'sp sync --open'.
- --force-if-includes semantics: it requires the local ref to include the remote's tip. Confirm this interacts correctly with spry's detached/plumbing pushes (pushBranch pushes '<sha>:refs/heads/<branch>', not a checked-out branch) - does --force-if-includes even apply meaningfully to a non-current-branch refspec push? If not, document why we still pass it (or drop it).
- Multi-machine / multi-actor: the whole point is clobber protection. Confirm a test that a concurrent remote force-push is still rejected AFTER checkSync's fetch (the regression spry-s2tx caught).
- Whether the pinned-lease baseline should come from checkSync's pre-fetch snapshot vs the PR-cache vs a fresh ls-remote - document the chosen source.

Deliverable: a short written finding (in this bead) confirming each push path is safe, plus any follow-up tasks it spawns. No code changes required by this bead unless the review finds a defect.

title: Task 7: share missing-ID scan with inject path
description:
## Task 7: Share the missing-ID scan with the inject path

**Files:**

- Modify: `src/git/rebase.ts`
- Test: `tests/commands/stack-analysis.test.ts` (already covers `missingIdHashes`)

`computeIdRewrites` in `rebase.ts` re-implements the "which commits lack an id" scan. Route it through the shared helper so there is one definition. `rebase.ts` scans `CommitInfo` (with `subject`/`body`, no parsed trailers), so add a thin adapter rather than changing `missingIdHashes`' input type.

- [ ] **Step 1: Confirm the current scan under test**

Run: `bun test tests/commands/rebase.test.ts`
Expected: PASS (baseline before refactor).

- [ ] **Step 2: Refactor `computeIdRewrites` to reuse the shared predicate**

In `src/git/rebase.ts`, the loop currently parses trailers per commit to find missing ids. Keep the per-commit `parseTrailers` (it needs the full message), but extract the boolean test into a named local that mirrors `missingIdHashes`' semantics, and add a comment linking them:

```ts
// Mirrors stack-analysis.missingIdHashes: a commit "needs an id" iff it has no
// Spry-Commit-Id trailer. Kept as a local test here because rebase parses the
// full commit message (CommitInfo has no pre-parsed trailers), whereas
// analyzeStack consumes already-parsed CommitWithTrailers.
const needsId = (trailers: Record<string, string>) => !trailers["Spry-Commit-Id"];
```

Then use `if (needsId(trailers)) missingIds.push(commit.hash);`. (This is the minimal DRY step that keeps one semantic definition without forcing rebase to re-parse into `CommitWithTrailers`.)

- [ ] **Step 3: Run rebase tests**

Run: `bun test tests/commands/rebase.test.ts`
Expected: PASS (behavior identical).

- [ ] **Step 4: Commit**

```bash
git add src/git/rebase.ts
git commit -m "refactor(rebase): name the missing-id predicate to mirror missingIdHashes

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>"
```

---
close_reason: Done: needsId named predicate mirrors missingIdHashes; behavior identical; 9/9 rebase; commit b293c95

title: Task 8: update land doc-test setup (open PRs already-stacked)
description:

Setup currently opens the upper PR on `main`. Under the new design land would fail that with a mis-targeted blocker. Open each PR on its correct stacked base so the stack is landable without any in-land write — and so setup never changes a PR base after CI starts.

- [ ] **Step 1: Change the `gh pr create --base` for the upper unit**
